# **\[Work-in-Progress\] Spark Cost Savings Road Map**

Status: *In progress*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: Nov 13, 2024  Last Updated: Nov 15, 2024

# Executive Summary

After executing the plan documented here,  [FY25 Spark Efficiency & Reliability Roadmap](https://docs.google.com/document/d/14-xglnAQ4woT7xkQuMb5TMH7wbDplgWhYfFQLjXUZMA/edit?tab=t.0), we have saved roughly $8 million dollars annually from March to October of 2024[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.v5lpegggd8s4) while improving the reliability by more than 67% at the same time[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.1uybypzugd4),  with roughly 1 engineering-year investment. We must continue investing in Spark cost savings through (1) short-term efforts to collaborate with users to improve the cost efficiency of existing jobs and consolidate resources[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.ap494raq68gr) for better performance, and (2) long-term efforts to manage capacity and maintain low costs over time. I estimate the ROI of these investments to be approximately $1 million in annual savings per engineering-year for the next $5 million annual savings opportunities.

# North Star

We will plan our road map with the following north star goal in mind:

* Infrastructure  
  * We will consolidate our Spark infrastructure into a smaller number of larger clusters. This reduces the waste caused by resource fragmentation, and also allows us to have better control with compute capacity when the EC2 capacity is scarce.  
  * We will manage the capacity with tiered queues to balance the SLA needs and cost.  
  * We will have a sophisticated capacity management system and process that can provide enough self-service-ability and also reduce risks of human errors.  
* User Experience  
  * We will have cost attributions to the Spark application level, enabling stakeholders to make decisions based on easier ROI calculations.  
  * We will have systems and processes in place to ensure that new jobs deployed to the production clusters meet the reliability and cost efficiency bar.

# High Level Strategy and Road Map

In this section, I will start with the high level strategy that will move the cost needle. I will break them down to projects in the road map section below.

After our FY25 investment, I believe we have captured most of the “low-hanging fruit” for cost savings, but we still have large savings opportunities. The biggest saving will come from merging a big number of small clusters into a small number of large ones. I estimate that we can save 15% if we can merge all clusters together[\[4\]](#bookmark=id.z0mxwrplc7dp). This represents an annual savings of $0.5 million for the 21 production clusters. We can save more when we are able to merge the backfill clusters as well, which requires long-term investment for automatic capacity management.

We should continue investing in improving, rewriting, or deleting existing Spark jobs. In **FY25Q2**, for the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster alone, we achieved a **30% cost reduction**[\[6\]](#bookmark=id.tz9lanvks0uw) through workload improvements and tuning. I anticipate similar cost-saving opportunities across other clusters that have not yet been addressed. I anticipate similar cost-saving opportunities across other clusters that have yet to be addressed. However, a significant challenge for this project is the need for greater collaboration and additional resources from multiple teams.

For instance, many data scientists and data engineers create their jobs in SQL using the Merlin framework. These users are not expected to have the expertise required to optimize their jobs for cost efficiency. The operational responsibility for these jobs falls to the Metrics team, but it is not scalable for them to optimize every individual workload, especially when they may lack the necessary business context. The Data Infrastructure team has the technical expertise to identify wasteful jobs, but we are more removed from the **business logic** and the **framework** that directly supports end users. Bridging these gaps will require coordinated efforts and a more integrated approach among the involved teams.

In FY25, we have been following through with the [FY25 Spark Efficiency & Reliability Roadmap](https://docs.google.com/document/d/14-xglnAQ4woT7xkQuMb5TMH7wbDplgWhYfFQLjXUZMA/edit?tab=t.0), and achieved $8 million annual cost savings for our Spark infrastructure while improving the Spark infrastructure reliability at the same time. Based on what we have learned in the past year, we recommend the following strategy and road map to continue the cost saving effort, with the goal to improve SLA (dataset landing time) and self-service user experience at the same time.

| What | Projects | Desired Outcome | ROI Estimate | Risk Level | Dependencies | Ideas, Notes and Next Steps |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Dynamic dashboard to attribute cost per Spark task | 1\. Create dynamic dashboards to attribute the cost of each Spark job to its appropriate owner2\. Provide meaningful metrics regarding what’s influence the cost | 1.Spark users can use the dashboard to identify jobs with large cost saving and reliability improvement opportunities2\. Spark users can use the dashboard to prioritize engineering efforts3\. Gatekeeping new Spark jobs to avoid low quality jobs to enter production clusters | 0.5 engineer-year | Low | Depends on quality YARN metrics and Spark metrics | Already in progress. ATS doesn’t seem to offer dynamic dashboard capability, so we may have to use other options like tableau  |
| Deleting, Tuning Spark jobs for cost saving | 1.Deleting low ROI jobs2.Tuning over-provisioned jobs | 1\. Low ROI, and unmaintained jobs are deleted from the infrastructure.2\. Jobs with large waste are tuned for improved reliability and lower cost  | 3 engineer-year (aggregated across DI and job owner teams), $3 million additional annual savings | Medium | Depends on first version of per-task Spark cost attribution and related metrics | [FY25H2 Spark Cost and Reliability Plan](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nWRwbMCN2qTebxF1CdVqy6xBMemLOs58Lz1s9E3F17g/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit)[Fixing Unmaintained Spark Jobs Unsuitable for Production clusters](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nKxc_IoMLdnDAcDx9Y57DqWUc9fqqGgec249Kfu2p8k/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit) |
| Spark Infrastructure Tuning | 1.Tune default configurations2.Fix technical debt with large cost impact3\. Resource oversubscription | 1.Capture and materialize cost savings from infrastructure level tuning.2\. Automate manual steps that can result in unwanted wastes | $1 million annual savings | Medium |  | [Spark Infrastructure Capacity Management Road Map](https://docs.google.com/document/d/19POB0Wl93-gE2JCnpOZaqDVrIXymhZjquY1Hg2brSVM/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit)[Spark Efficiency & Reliability Roadmap](https://docs.google.com/document/d/14-xglnAQ4woT7xkQuMb5TMH7wbDplgWhYfFQLjXUZMA/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit) |
| Merging Spark Clusters | 1.Merge clusters into smaller number of larger clusters2\. Intelligent capacity management control plane | 1.Ideally,we just need two clusters, one with secure data access and one without secure data access | 1 engineer-year, 15% cost savings for merged clusters, $0.5 million a year savings | Medium |  |  |
| Rate Card | 1.Develop rate card to establish a standard and transparent pricing model for resource usage across different users, teams, and SLA tiers | 1.Spark users understand how the job cost is estimated, building trust for the cost attribution | 0.25 engineer-year | Extremely Low | Depends on  | TBD |
| Spark Auto-Tuning | 1.Add near-real-time metrics for Spark jobs by adopting Uber’s JVM-Profiler2\. Developing automatic Spark configuration tuning based on near real-time metrics. | 1.Spark jobs with predictable resource usage profile will be tuned automatically.2\. Fallback to existing user configuration when auto tuning fails | 2 engineer-year, $2 million dollar annual savings (based on Uber’s result) | High | TBD | We can develop either an online (higher risk) or an offline (lower-risk) tuning service to calculate the configurations for the next run.  |

# Top Risks, Unknowns and Callouts

# Ownership Dependencies & Call to Action

# F.A.Q

# Appendix

## \[1\] We saved nearly 70%, or $8.23 million annually, on the 21 most expensive clusters from February to October

The following chart shows the cost saving progress for the most expensive 21 clusters from February to October. In February these clusters cost $981829 in total. In October, the cost was $295,906. The total saving rate comes to 69.86% (or $8.23 million dollar annual savings).

![][image1]

## \[2\] Spark infrastructure reliability has improved significantly and no longer a concern

The Spark infrastructure reliability has significantly improved during FY25Q1 and Q2, through root-causing and fixing infrastructure issues. As of FY25Q3, the Spark infrastructure reliability is no longer a concern. Please see details in [2025 Q1, Q2 EMR/Spark Reliability ](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1vEQ5UD_r-cnqmI33CBxIDprPv074OHdYe_09wiZZeMY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit)

## \[3\] We must consolidate our clusters for both cost and reliability

Industry best practices indicate that a smaller number of larger clusters provides greater cost efficiency. For example, LinkedIn operates a single, large logical YARN cluster with 15,000 machines, powered by two physical clusters of 7,500 machines each. Currently, we run a large number of small clusters based on a past decision that assumed team-based clusters would be more reliable and easier to manage costs. While this setup does improve cost transparency—allowing us to view cluster expenses through AWS Cost Explorer—it is inefficient to operate small clusters that autoscale independently. Consolidating resources and reducing fragmentation would better serve Slack’s interests.

Independently autoscaled clusters are wasteful because it’s impossible to set an upper limit on the total number of machines across multiple clusters scaling independently. This prevents us from purchasing a Savings Plan during peak usage hours without incurring significant waste. Additionally, when each cluster scales up or down independently, resources are underutilized during transitions, resulting in a low usage ratio on machines. Finally, providing a capacity guarantee with On-Demand Capacity Reservations (ODCR) is challenging with a large number of independently autoscaled clusters.

Independently autoscaled clusters are less reliable because they rely heavily on spot instances, which can be terminated with only a 2-minute notice, leading to job failures and frequent retries. There is also a significant risk when a cluster scales up from a cold state (with a minimal number of machines) or when AWS cannot provide enough spot instances—or even on-demand instances—to meet demand.

Independently autoscaled clusters also create unnecessary team toil. Clusters can end up competing for EC2 resources, and it becomes impractical for the infrastructure team to investigate each scenario when users report that one cluster is receiving resources while another is not. See this [escal](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C01FB307Z7A/p1731346543144069?thread_ts=1730978274.801519&cid=C01FB307Z7A) as an example.

See more context about the urgency of consolidating clusters in this [announcement](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C8EDM9MGA/p1726601113255819).

## \[4\] We can save 15% from merging clusters

I estimate that we can save 20% for each merged cluster as shown in Appendix [\[5\]](#bookmark=id.qvr8v060k1ue). Based on this assumption, I estimated 15% total savings from merging all clusters.  Using the daily cost on November 14 as an example (based on AWS [Cost Explorer](https://us-east-1.console.aws.amazon.com/costmanagement/home?region=us-east-1#/cost-explorer?chartStyle=STACK&costAggregate=netAmortizedCost&endDate=2024-11-15&excludeForecasting=false&filter=%5B%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22CostCategoryName%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Cost%20category%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Data%20Infrastructure%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Automatic%20-%20Omni%20Team%20Name%22%7D%7D,%7B%22dimension%22:%7B%22id%22:%22TagKey%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Tag%22%7D,%22operator%22:%22INCLUDES%22,%22values%22:%5B%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform4-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform4-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-platform3-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dma-temp-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dma-temp-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-etl-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-etl-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-odin-tools-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-odin-tools-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-odin-tools2-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-odin-tools2-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-odin-tools3-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-odin-tools3-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-dea-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-dea-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-search-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-search-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-infra-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-infra-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-tools-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-tools-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-insights-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-insights-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-interop-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-interop-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-sqooper-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-sqooper-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-data-ingestion-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-data-ingestion-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-percolator-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-percolator-emr6%22%7D,%7B%22value%22:%22prod-security-emr6%22,%22displayValue%22:%22prod-security-emr6%22%7D%5D,%22growableValue%22:%7B%22value%22:%22Name%22,%22displayValue%22:%22Name%22%7D%7D%5D&futureRelativeRange=CUSTOM&granularity=Daily&groupBy=%5B%22TagKeyValue:Name%22%5D&historicalRelativeRange=CUSTOM&isDefault=false&reportArn=arn:aws:ce::429538831549:ce-saved-report%2F1a327863-5a1b-4929-844b-eaafa0407948&reportId=1a327863-5a1b-4929-844b-eaafa0407948&reportName=New%20cost%20and%20usage%20report&showOnlyUncategorized=false&showOnlyUntagged=false&startDate=2024-11-13&usageAggregate=undefined&useNormalizedUnits=false)), the total cost for the 21 clusters was $8,800, with the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster accounting for $2,200 of this amount. Assuming we merge 20 clusters into the prod-data-platform-emr6 cluster and save 20% from each merged cluster, the total savings would amount to approximately 15%.

## \[5\] We can save 20% for each merged cluster

Running independently autoscaled clusters creates the following waste:

* **Waste from autoscaling.** When scaling up a machine, it can take up to 10 minutes to spin it up. During this time, the machine is idle, yet we are paying for those 10 minutes. Once the machine is operational, it may not be fully utilized immediately, leading to inefficient resource usage and waste.  
  * Similarly, during scaling down, machines are terminated only after the workload has been drained. This process can take a significant amount of time, during which the machines operate with low resource utilization, further contributing to waste.  
  * For example, long-running **Merlin backfill jobs** often utilize only a small number of cores, but they run for extended periods. This combination of low resource utilization and prolonged job duration exacerbates inefficiency during both scaling up and scaling down processes.  
* **Waste from EC2 instance types**  
  * **Spot Instances**. When using spot instances, they can be interrupted with as little as 2 minutes’ notice, a situation commonly referred to as a **spot interruption**. When this occurs:  
    * Jobs running on the interrupted machine may fail and need to be rerun, wasting all the computation completed up to that point (if the application master is running on that machine).  
    * Tasks in progress on the interrupted machine will need to be rescheduled and rerun on another machine, which itself could also face interruption.  
    * Additionally, tasks that have already completed might need to be rerun if shuffle files stored on the interrupted machine are lost, potentially causing **stage-level reruns**.  
  * On-Demand Instances. For machines using on-demand instances, it becomes challenging to cap the maximum number of instances across independently scaling clusters. As a result, instances exceeding the coverage provided by the Savings Plan will incur costs that are **twice as high**, significantly increasing expenses.  
* **Waste from resource fragmentations.** Cluster A may have spare resources while Cluster B requires additional resources. However, due to fragmentation, the spare resources in Cluster A cannot be utilized by the workload in Cluster B. As a result, each cluster must independently initiate the autoscaling process, leading to inefficient and wasteful resource usage.

Using our most cost-efficient cluster, prod-data-platform-emr6, as an example, this cluster achieves its efficiency by saving 80% of its costs and maintaining the highest resource utilization at the YARN scheduler level. However, despite its cost efficiency, it still exhibits resource underutilization that could be addressed by merging clusters.  
For instance, the chart below highlights a 3-hour window where resource peaks indicate significant spare capacity within the cluster. Specifically, during the 15-minute window from **6:36 to 6:51**, the cluster’s resource utilization was at just **50%**. By merging clusters, we could utilize such periods of underused capacity to run lower-priority workloads, maximizing efficiency and reducing waste.

![][image2]

## \[6\] We saved 80% cost for data-platform clusters from March to October

For the data-platform clusters, we began with four clusters costing **$459,595** in February. Eight months later, we achieved a stable monthly cost of **$89,833**, representing an **80% reduction**.

The chart below, sourced from AWS Cost Explorer, illustrates the month-over-month cost for the data-platform clusters. Most of the cost savings in **Q1 (March to April)** resulted from merging these four clusters into a single cluster, replacing spot instances with more reliable on-demand instances, and utilizing Savings Plans and ODCR (On-Demand Capacity Reservations) for further cost optimization.

Much of the savings in **Q2**, from **$144,286 in April** to **$99,177 in July**, were achieved by manually identifying and optimizing existing Spark jobs.

![][image3]

—- END —-

\[1\] Cost Saving Opportunity Sizing

The next stage of cost savings comes from identifying and optimizing the existing Spark jobs. Due to resource constraints, the majority of our Spark workload is not optimized for resource efficiency. To get an estimate of the size of further cost saving opportunities, we should look at the data-platform clusters as an example.

In February before we start the cost saving journey in earnest, we are running four clusters for data-platforms, with a total cost of $459,595. By the end of April, we had finished a couple of major efforts, by combining the four clusters into one cluster to reduce the resource fragmentation, and also replaced the spot instances with on-demand instances covered with Savings Plan and ODCR. As you can see from the AWS Cost Explorer chart below, from March to October, we were able to save more than 80% of the cost. The majority of cost savings from Q2 comes from manually identifying and working with the owners (the data ingestion team) to optimize the jobs. This accounted for 31% cost savings from April ($144286) to July ($99177). 

We were able to capture the same percentage of cost savings in Q1 for the second most expensive cluster for the DMA team, which is responsible for supporting business critical metrics and experimentations. However, we were not able to capture the same saving rate in Q2 through identifying and optimizing individual jobs. The main reasons are the following:

* It’s easier to work directly with the data-ingestion team on their data pipelines because they fully own these jobs. However, the same approach doesn’t apply to the DMA cluster, as the direct owners of these workloads lack the skills to tune Spark jobs—and they shouldn’t need to acquire them. It’s not the best use of data scientists’ time to learn job optimization for efficiency. The Metric Foundation team is responsible for the landing time of a large number of metrics, but it is a difficult task for them to tune each job individually without the knowledge of business requirements.  
* A large portion of our Spark workloads (TODO: add percentage of Merlin jobs) are SQL-based jobs. Currently, resources for these workloads are assigned using a t-shirt sizing model, which is highly inefficient. In May 2024, a significant number of workloads were oversized simply because the driver ran out of memory. It’s unrealistic to ask end users to tune their workloads for cost efficiency, and it’s also not scalable for the infrastructure team (DI or MFA) to manually tune each job to reduce waste.

If we can find ways to solve this above problem, we expect to reach the same total savings (80%) for all clusters, or, 30% incremental savings by optimizing existing workload. Using the 21 most expensive clusters as an example, they cost a total of $981829 in February, and $295909 in October, which account for a total of 69.8% savings.
